input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a lab-scale gantry crane with a controller, designed for research and educational purposes in a controlled laboratory environment. Key characteristics include its open-source nature, reproducibility, and suitability for validating digital twin methodologies such as continuous validation, fault detection, and system evolution notation (DarTwin).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including optimal control for crane operations, historical data logging for performance tracking, real-time data visualization for monitoring, continuous validation to ensure model accuracy against physical data, and fault detection for anomaly identification. These services support researchers and educators in validating digital twin methodologies such as continuous validation, fault detection, and system evolution notation (DarTwin).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Uses C4 Model for architecture visualization and description, including context and container diagrams for the gantry crane system components and their relationships.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Ad-hoc integration approach leveraging containerization for deployment, without reliance on a commercial digital twin framework.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates CAD models and a kinematic model of the gantry crane. The Trajectory Generator Service utilizes the kinematic model for path computation, while a TimescaleDB database stores all system-generated data for temporal analysis and retrieval.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Two motors with associated controllers manage cart movement and hoisting motion, respectively. An electromagnet at the end of the hoist enables container pickup, with actuation ranges constrained by mechanical limits and safety interlocks to prevent operational hazards.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: A Continuous Validation Service compares executed trajectory measurements against simulated trajectories, calculates distance metrics, and compares values against predefined thresholds to detect discrepancies. This ensures model fidelity through automated error detection and correction mechanisms, with regular validation against physical system data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the physical crane is collected via the Physical Crane Interface (Python program connecting to crane controllers) and continuously logged to a TimescaleDB database by the Logging Application. Specific details on data types, transmission frequencies, event triggers, preprocessing steps, and integration mechanisms between these components are not explicitly provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application sends movement commands (e.g., target positions and velocities) to the Physical Crane Interface, which translates these into controller-specific commands for the physical crane. The Trajectory Generator Service provides optimized trajectories for movement, which the Control Application executes. Feedback from the physical crane is monitored by the Continuous Validation Service, which compares simulated and actual trajectories and triggers alerts if discrepancies exceed acceptable thresholds.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for signaling between digital twin services via an MQTT Broker.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The system is deployed on-premise on a personal computer, with TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto, and Grafana containerized using Docker. This deployment model utilizes Docker for service isolation and management, though computational, storage, scalability, and availability specifics beyond the PC-based setup are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Air Quality Management exemplar follows Model-Driven digital twin engineering methodologies [13], while GreenhouseDT employs self-adaptive approaches. The lab scale gantry crane utilizes the C4 Model [5] for architecture visualization. All exemplars use GitHub for version control, containerization/virtualization technologies for dissemination, and adopt an ad-hoc development approach without commercial digital twin frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management exemplar provides three core applications: runtime visualization (e.g., dashboard for real-time monitoring), simulation for scenario testing, and prediction for forecasting conditions, enabling informed decision-making in virus spread prevention within construction engineering contexts.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system applies the C4 Model [5] for standardized software architecture visualization and description, with no additional industry-specific standards explicitly documented for the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The system utilizes a Continuous Validation Service to monitor operational thresholds; when thresholds are breached, operators are notified via the dashboard to take appropriate action. Fail-safe mechanisms are referenced within the digital twin services.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the lab-scale gantry crane, its purpose, and the specific research methodologies it supports, which are all explicitly detailed in the source text. It correctly identifies the system's open-source and reproducible nature.
quotes: lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin
demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods, fault detection methods and DarTwin

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction lists the specific services provided by the digital twin, matching the abstract and the architecture section perfectly. It includes optimal control, data logging, visualization, and continuous validation, and accurately identifies the research use cases supported by these services.
quotes: services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation
Dashboard: a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data
Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

The document mentions that 'During operation data flows from the actual to the digital system' and describes services for continuous validation and data logging. However, it does not provide specific details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, time scales for services, or temporal granularity. Therefore, the claim that this characteristic is not present is accurate.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The document describes a single lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin. It refers to 'the physical gantry crane including its controller' and 'the crane’s digital twin' as singular instances. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins. Therefore, the claim is accurate.

The extraction accurately describes the use of the C4 Model for architecture visualization. The text states: 'In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and describe the architecture of the system with a context and container diagram.' It further details the components in the context and container diagrams (e.g., 'Physical Crane', 'Database', 'Trajectory Generator Service'). The extraction correctly captures this orchestration and architectural description.

The extraction correctly identifies the ad-hoc integration approach and use of containerization. The text states: 'we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.' It also notes: 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.' Additionally, under 'Digital Twin Frameworks', it confirms: 'Our approach is much more ad-hoc... We make use of open-source technologies, but none of them are tailored specifically to digital twins.'

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the digital twin's composition and services. It correctly identifies CAD models, the kinematic model, the specific role of the Trajectory Generator Service, and the TimescaleDB database used for logging. Supported by quotes: 'The digital side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane', 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories', 'Database: a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data created by the system'.

The description of motors and the electromagnet is precise and directly supported by the source. However, the mention of 'safety interlocks' is speculative and not mentioned in the text, reducing the score slightly. Supported by quotes: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement', 'An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers'.

The description of the Continuous Validation Service is highly accurate regarding the comparison of trajectories and threshold checking. However, it slightly misrepresents the process by claiming 'automated error correction', whereas the text states operators are notified to take action. Supported by quotes: 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations', 'It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the components involved in the data flow (Physical Crane Interface, Logging Application, TimescaleDB) and accurately states that the document lacks specific technical details like frequencies or data types. Supported by quotes: 'Physical Crane Interface: ... a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane' and 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.'

The description accurately details the control flow via the Control Application and Physical Crane Interface, supported by the text. The inclusion of the Trajectory Generator Service and Continuous Validation Service is well-grounded, though the direct link where the Control Application executes trajectories provided by the Generator is implied rather than explicitly stated. The feedback mechanism via the Continuous Validation Service is described in depth. Supported by quotes: 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane' and 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories.'

The extraction correctly identifies the protocol and its function within the system. The source describes the container 'MQTT Broker' as 'an MQTT broker for signaling between services' and specifies that 'the ubiquitous and open-source Eclipse Mosquitto was used'.

The extraction is highly specific, accurately describing the on-premise personal computer deployment and the use of Docker for specific services (Database, MQTT Broker, Dashboard). It correctly identifies the limitations regarding scalability and availability details present in the text. Supported by quotes: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer' and 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.'

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
